As a society, we place an extremely high value on time. Time as we know it is a critical part of our lives. It affects what type of clothing we wear, our shopping habits, the food we eat, the shows we watch, and much more. Deadlines cause anxiety because of the perception of the loss you'll incur in the future, or more accurately, your vision of the future. You become a hostage to the future. In this case, a hostage to your vision of how you hope things will play out. Great negotiators understand the perceptions that deadlines create and use that understanding in their negotiations. Time is your ally if you choose it to be. In business, deadlines are often arbitrary, nearly always flexible, and hardly ever trigger the consequences we think or are told they will. The purpose of deadlines, the vast majority of the time, is to simply incite progress. Deadlines are used in negotiation because universally we know it adds pressure. Whether or not you know it's tied to loss is another question. That said, subconsciously, we know loss is a strong motivator. In general, yourself and others don't know any other ways to apply said pressure without stumbling through a time constraint. Most of the anxiety and stress we feel from deadlines are self-inflicted figments of our imagination. We've alluded to the emotional impact of self-inflicted thoughts on our imagination over and over. Human beings' default mode is hardwired to be overly negative. There's three times as much space in the amygdala devoted to negative emotions as opposed to positive ones. Many scientific studies on the impact of negative thoughts on our emotional reactions to loss tend to bear this ratio out. This simultaneously reveals a common sense reality that is hard to ignore. When you feel the pressure from a deadline, you have taken yourself hostage, and you often create an environment of reactive behaviors and poor choices, which tips to scales in the favor of your counterpart. It isn't easy to resist the urge to give into deadlines. The current mentality that is pervasive in business today is that revealing your deadline to your counterpart makes you vulnerable. It is seen as a weakness. However, this is wrong. Any weakness or vulnerability is in the eye of the beholder and ultimately hinges on your own perspective, your own agreement to be taken hostage by it. Any weakness can be flipped to be a condition of proceeding. You'd relay it as, if you don't solve this for me, I can't make a deal. If you don't solve this for me, by the deadline, I have to move on. Deadlines affect both parties involved. The key is that when negotiation is over for one side, it's over for the other one. If you hide your cutoff, it allows the other side to think they have more time. This encourages an action on their part. If you reveal your deadline to your counterpart as something working against them, you can actually get better deals. If your counterpart is unwilling to reveal their deadline, there are subtle ways to determine if you're getting close to said date. No matter what type of negotiation you're in, pay attention to how specifically they address these four key areas. Who, what, when, and how in referring to their pressures. If your counterpart increases the specificity, it indicates their feeling increased pressure. It is also important to remember that deadlines are primarily emotional, which means when you feel you're approaching them, you tend to make bad choices. It can trick you into believing that doing a deal now is more important than getting a good deal. If you cannot agree on a deal, if you're not willing to walk away, then it's you who has been taken hostage. I promise you, no deal is better than a bad deal. A bad deal will bleed you for years. When you finally internalize this liberating mantra, your patience becomes a very powerful weapon. How do you deal with a deadline that someone's placed on you? Is a series of skills that we have discussed up to this point in order to deal with deadlines put on you? Number one, it seems like you're worried about how much progress we've made. Number two, it sounds like if this isn't done by X time, there's no way this will work. Number three, it seems like there isn't any time to get this right. Four, what should be the plan if we miss the mark? And five, what happens if we pass the deadline?
